behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files in the repository are excluded from scanning.
behaviors.justification: Testing this requires wiring file discovery (from GitHub API or git state) together with the filtering logic. A Unit test of just the filter function wouldn't catch integration failures: the API could be called incorrectly, the filter could be bypassed in the orchestration, or the changed-file list could be mishandled. Integration testing with a mocked GitHub API response (or real git history) is necessary to verify the filter is applied correctly across the full pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a git repo with 10 files, commit changes to only 3 of them, mock the GitHub PR context to report those 3 as changed, run the pipeline, and verify via test instrumentation that the analysis step received exactly those 3 files (not all 10). Capture which files the analyzer was invoked with.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test verifies the filter is wired into the analysis step. A Unit test of filter logic alone wouldn't catch if the filter is applied at the wrong stage or not applied at all.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment with correct formatting and ordering.
behaviors.justification: While grouping logic could be unit-tested in isolation, the real failure boundary includes serialization into the GitHub comment format and the API call itself. A Unit test of just the grouping function wouldn't catch bugs like: incorrect markdown formatting in the comment, missing or malformed severity headers, wrong payload structure sent to GitHub API. Integration with a mocked GitHub API call is needed to verify the full data transformation and posting flow.
behaviors.test_description: Run the analysis with mock findings of mixed severities, capture the GitHub API call payload (via mock), and assert that the comment body contains findings correctly grouped by severity with proper markdown formatting, correct section headers, and correct ordering within each group.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test checks the actual comment format that gets posted, not just the grouping function. Mocking the GitHub API call is necessary to inspect what would be posted.

behaviors.description: Analysis of 200 files completes within the 10-minute GitHub Actions timeout, including file I/O and linter subprocess execution.
behaviors.justification: Performance under load cannot be tested with Unit mocks that return instant results—this would hide real bottlenecks in file I/O, linter speed, or algorithm complexity. Real file I/O and realistic linter execution (real subprocess or carefully-timed simulation) is necessary. System testing might be needed if the Actions environment applies resource throttling, but Integration with real files and subprocesses should catch most failures (slow algorithms, N² complexity, unoptimized I/O).
behaviors.test_description: Create a test directory with 200 files of varied sizes and complexity (mix of small config files, medium code files, large files). Run the full pipeline (file discovery, analysis, result assembly, API call). Instrument with timing and assert the entire operation completes in under 10 minutes. Use a real or realistically-timed linter (do not mock analysis as instant).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Performance testing requires real file I/O and real execution timing. Pure in-process mocks would hide the actual bottleneck and always pass.

behaviors.description: When the linter crashes on a malformed file, the pipeline catches the error, continues analyzing remaining files, and posts the partial results.
behaviors.justification: Error handling and recovery cannot be adequately tested by mocking a linter crash—real or realistic crash behavior is necessary. A Unit mock might simulate a generic exception, but real linter crashes may produce specific exception types, corrupt state, or require subprocess cleanup. Integration testing with an actual malformed file and realistic linter failure (real subprocess or a detailed crash simulation) is needed to verify the pipeline doesn't exit early, handles state correctly, and posts partial results. System testing would be needed if subprocess-specific OS behavior (signals, cleanup) matters.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test with 5 files, intentionally make one malformed (e.g., invalid syntax or truncated), run the analysis with a real or realistic linter, verify the malformed file is caught as an error, the other 4 files are successfully analyzed, and the final posted results include findings from the 4 successful files with a notation that 1 file had an error.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing with a real malformed file and realistic error handling is necessary. Unit mocking might miss subprocess state corruption or realistic crash behavior.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output—only one occurrence appears in the final result.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is pure data transformation with no external I/O, network calls, or subprocess dependencies. A Unit test with constructed mock finding objects is sufficient to verify the algorithm. The format of findings is controlled by the code, so there's no risk that real findings will surprise the dedup logic. Integration testing adds no value here—it would only repeat the same dedup logic in a more complex context.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock finding objects representing the same line flagged by two different overlapping rules (e.g., two rules both detecting an unused variable). Pass the list to the deduplication logic and assert the output contains exactly one finding for that line. Test edge cases: same line/message/severity but different rule names; same line but different severities.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Deduplication has no external dependencies. Unit testing with carefully chosen mock data is sufficient and appropriate.
